chapters.title: Trouble arrives with a companion called change
chapters.text:
Toru Okada had a wife and no job. He had left his seat at a law office where he had worked since college. The work paid the bills, but he wanted to try something else. He could afford to wait while looking for a new path, because Kumiko, his wife, brought home solid pay as a magazine editor and freelance artist.

A few weeks passed and small fights began to break out at home. On one lazy afternoon, while doing housework, Toru picked up a strange phone call from a woman. The voice was teasing and vaguely sexual. Looking for something to take his mind off it, Toru walked out to search for their missing cat in a nearby empty lane, but he came back empty handed. After the cat vanished, Kumiko also slipped out one morning, leaving no note. Toru could not line up all that had just happened.

So begins a long search that pulls Toru in directions he never planned. Along the way he learns who Mr Honda was before people called him a spiritual reader. Lieutenant Mamiya, an old friend of Mr Honda from the war in Manchuria, tells a long story about Honda, and the conversation pulls Toru into the history of Japan in the second world war.

In the meantime, Toru keeps meeting odd characters whose lives quietly overlap. Nutmeg Akaska, one of these people, has a father who shares the curious mark on Toru cheek. People who have heard the call of the strange mechanical bird tend to meet sad ends.

Mystery draws the curious in. If you want people to leave you alone, leave nothing hidden. The reverse holds as well.

If you are wondering how the story ends, whether Toru finds the cat and his wife, keep reading. We will follow the bird together.

chapters.title: A sweet marriage turned cold
chapters.text:
Toru and Kumiko had been married for six years when he lost his job. With time on his hands, Toru took stock of his career and his marriage. He took the relationship apart in his mind, piece by piece.

Life had seemed normal until that point, apart from the strange calls from a woman who asked for ten minutes of his time. The caller was Malta Kano. After some nudging from Kumiko, Toru agreed to meet her, and she turned out to be a psychic. Malta told him about a sister of hers called Creta, who claimed she had been assaulted by Noboru Wataya, Toru brother in law. Malta went on to say that Toru life was about to shift, and she pointed to the missing cat as the start of that shift.

Tangible details catch the eye but rarely amount to much. They are sideshows. The further you try to look, the broader the picture gets.

The Wataya family, Kumiko side, had a habit of consulting mediums and seers. The visit with Malta Kano brought Mr Honda back into Toru mind. Honda had been a spirit channeler whom Kumiko father had asked the couple to meet before the wedding. After meeting Malta, Toru noticed that both Honda and Malta had spoken to him about water. Honda had told him to be careful. Malta had spent years in austerities on the island of Malta, which gave her the first part of her name, and she had gone there to study water. Perhaps it was a coincidence, but both of them warned him about water, and the warning lodged in his chest.

chapters.title: The story of May Kasahara and Creta Kano
chapters.text:
A small grove of trees stood near the Okada house, and from it came a mechanical call that sounded like a bird winding a spring. The couple named it the wind-up bird, a label Kumiko had given it, and although they never saw the bird, they knew its sound well.

While looking for the missing cat in the area, Toru met a teenage girl named May Kasahara who lived near some empty plots nearby. For a sixteen year old, Kasahara was a dark sort, always dragging every topic back to death. When Toru asked about a scar on her face and the way she limped, she told him about a motorcycle crash. She later admitted that the limp was a habit she had kept even after she had healed.

A person company at any given moment often reflects where the heart sits.

Toru had a brush with death when Kasahara left him alone at the bottom of a dry well on the unused land where they often met. He had climbed down there to think about how low things had gone since Kumiko left. Kasahara later said sorry. Toru escaped in a way he could not explain, with help from Creta Kano.

Toru first met Creta in his own house. She had come at the request of her sister Malta to collect water samples from the Okada tap. During that visit, Creta told Toru a long story about her life. Their second meeting happened in a dream, and the dream was vivid. They made love in that dream more than once. What made it strange was that Creta later described how that encounter felt to her, in detail, when they next met. Toru could not place the moment. Was it a hallucination, or had it really happened?

According to one detective agency, the large majority of people admit they would cheat if they knew they would never be caught.

chapters.title: A close look at the day Kumiko walked out
chapters.text:
Mr Honda, the old spiritual reader for the Okada family, died while Toru life was unraveling. When he came by to deliver a keepsake, Lieutenant Mamiya sat with Toru and told him long stories about the time he and Honda had spent at the front during the war. The keepsake came in a small box, and the Lieutenant told Toru to open it only after Honda had died. After the Lieutenant left, Toru found the box empty, and he sat staring at it for a long time.

War reshapes nations and reshapes the people and animals inside them.

The morning Kumiko left, there was no warning. While he was zipping up the back of her dress, Toru noticed she wore a new perfume. He asked about it kindly, and she left without a word, as though she was already late. Toru stayed up most of that night waiting for her to come home. She never did. The next morning he called her office and learned she had not shown up. She never did return, and Toru had Kasahara call the office days later to be sure.

Trust in another person is one of the finest things we can hold on to, whether it pays off or not.

Toru later found the perfume case, a Christian Dior box, with a ribbon around it. It looked like a gift from a man. Interestingly, Kumiko had taken none of her clothes with her, not a single shoe or change of outfit. Toru would later learn she had stopped by the laundromat that morning to pick up a set of clothes he had dropped off.

While Toru sat at home waiting for any word on Kumiko, Malta called and invited him to a meeting with Kumiko and Noboru Wataya. When the three of them sat down at the Shinagawa Pacific Hotel, Noboru claimed that his sister Kumiko had asked for a formal end to her marriage to Toru.

chapters.title: Toru descent into darkness and Creta return
chapters.text:
Toru took Mr Honda advice literally and went down into the deep, dry well on the unused land near his home. In the silence and the dark, he thought through his life. He tried to pin down the moment when things had begun to slip between him and Kumiko, and he landed on the day she went in for an abortion.

When you need to climb, find the tallest tower. When you need to fall, find the deepest well and go all the way down.

Toru packed a bag, walked to the well, and lowered himself on a long rope ladder tied to a tree at the top. He brought only a bottle of water, and hunger found him quickly. He would have climbed back up the same way, but Kasahara, who had shown him the well in the first place, pulled up the ladder once he had passed out. She might have left him there. Creta woke him and lowered the ladder back down.

When Toru came up, Creta was gone. He walked home, ate, washed up, and slept. Not long after, Malta called asking if he had seen her sister. Toru hurried back to the well and found Creta sitting at the bottom. She told him not to worry, she was there to think, just as he had been.

Time alone sharpens the mind and loosens the hold of old feelings.

Toru went home and returned to the well the next morning. It was empty. While shaving the beard he had grown, he noticed a dark blue mark on his right cheek, the same cheek that grew warm during his long trances at the bottom of the well. Malta had asked him on her last call if he had noticed any change to his body.

chapters.title: A turning point that changed the path
chapters.text:
Toru realized he had not checked the mail since he came back from his time in the well. When he did, he found a letter in Kumiko handwriting. She tried to explain why she had left, her guilt over the affair, how she felt about the abortion years before, and a request that he stop looking for her.

A single wrong step can change the shape of a calm future.

He went to bed that night shaken. In the morning he woke up to find a woman lying naked on Kumiko side of the bed. It was Creta. She picked up the story of her life where she had left off. Creta stayed for several days, wore Kumiko clothes, cooked for him, and they were together in the flesh, a replay of their dream. She invited him to come with her to Crete, the island she had always wanted to visit. Toru turned her down after talking it over with his uncle, Mr Tsuruta.

Months passed and Toru drifted. The Wataya family kept pressing him to sign the divorce papers, but he refused. He wanted to see Kumiko first. Their pressure grew into threats, and this went on for nearly a year. Around that time, Toru went back to the alley that led to the old Hanging House and found the lot had been torn down. Kasahara was nowhere to be seen.

Toru called his uncle and asked for the contact of the real estate agent who handled several plots in the Setagaya area. When he visited the agent, Mr Ichikawa, Toru asked what had happened to the plot where the Miyawaki family had lived. Ichikawa explained that a foreign investor had bought the land to flip it for a profit, but no one wanted it because of the dark history tied to the property.

chapters.title: Coming back to the surface, a fresh start
chapters.text:
Mr Ichikawa told Toru that the asking price for the land was about eighty million yen, given that no buyers had stepped up.

A second thread came out of the call with his uncle. Toru needed to rebuild a normal life by paying attention to small things. Once he sat with that, he realized he had no one to talk to anymore. The Kasahara family had moved away. So Toru started to walk. At first he stuck to the blocks around Setagaya, then he took the train to Shinjuku Station and wandered. It was there that Nutmeg Akasaka first noticed him, and a friendship began. Nutmeg was not her real name, and her son name was not Cinnamon either.

Life is what we make of the raw material it hands us, good or bad.

Nutmeg and Cinnamon noticed the mark on Toru right cheek. Nutmeg later told him her father had carried the same mark. As a sharp designer with expensive taste, Nutmeg gave Toru a complete style overhaul. Soon he was running a fitting service for wealthy middle-aged women.

Spend cash on what money can buy. Spend time on what it cannot.

Nutmeg and Cinnamon bought the empty lot, built a small elegant house on it, and deepened the well even though it was still dry. They added a steel ladder. Toru came to the rebuilt property through the back alley of his own house. Only Cinnamon used the gated front. He drove a black Mercedes and brought his clients in through the same hidden way, so the neighbors had no idea who owned the place or what went on behind the tall walls. Toru spent nights in the well and days in the house, and went home now and then.

chapters.title: Where imagination meets the real world
chapters.text:
Through Mr Ushikawa, a quiet spy, Noboru Wataya learned that Toru was the new owner of the Hanging House. Noboru tried to bargain with Toru, offering to buy the property in return for a chance to speak with Kumiko. The aim was to protect his political standing. Noboru offered to clear the eighty million yen mortgage.

Mr Ushikawa then pitched Toru a deal to chat with Kumiko online through a computer. In time, Nutmeg and Cinnamon traced Noboru and Toru back to the speculative press about the Hanging House. They cut their ties to Toru and the lot without warning.

Toru stayed away from the property for six straight days. The sixth day, the silence drove him back. Knowing he might lose the place now that the fitting work had ended, he climbed down the well one last time. Down there, he slipped into a trance and found himself in the same room he had visited on his earlier trips.

The mind can shape what the hand eventually builds.

In that room, Toru met a woman who spoke with many voices, and one of them was Kumiko. Then a second figure appeared and lunged at Toru with a knife, leaving cuts on his left shoulder and right cheek. Toru fought back in the trance with a baseball bat and cracked open the attacker skull.

When the trance cleared, Toru was back at the bottom of the well and the well was full of water. The cuts and the aches from the trance were still on his body. The water started to rise and Toru would have drowned if Cinnamon Akasaka had not pulled him out at the last moment.

chapters.title: Final thoughts
chapters.text:
Toru first blamed his troubles on quitting his job without a plan. His early meeting with Mr Honda had made him doubt anything that smelled of the supernatural.

What had happened to Creta at the hands of Noboru Wataya echoed what later trapped Kumiko. The affairs Kumiko had before leaving her husband were clearly beyond her control. The same was true of Kumiko sister, who died young.

After she left the Setagaya area, May Kasahara grew close to the wind-up bird man, Toru. Toru and Creta drifted apart over time, though Creta eventually moved to Japan, against her old wish to settle in Crete.

Toru time in the well, the mark on his cheek, and a chain of strange events all pushed him, slowly and without his noticing, toward Kumiko. In the end, his instincts had pulled him along the path he needed to walk to free Kumiko from her brother grip. The ending of the book feels open and quiet, but at least Toru held on to his life and his mind.

Memories and thoughts age, just as people do. But some thoughts never get old and some memories never fade.

Toru was the only person who heard the dark call of the wind-up bird. No one ever saw the bird, and the story hints that those who hear it meet grim ends. So the bird stands for the strange trials each person carries and the lonely weight that comes with them.

Try this. Look around at the people close to you today. Appreciate them for who they are. Try to understand them well enough that no outside voice can come between you.
